text:
For SCU physics alumni Craig Benko ’10 and Bryan Berggren ’17, hands-on research, close mentorship, and long hours solving tough problems helped shape careers in the fast-moving world of photonics.

Click the link below to read their full story. ⬇️ 🤩 

https://t.co/gMLOkQNcFE

Yttrium 

Y, it’s atomic number 39.  It’s a silvery white rare earth metal that is abundant, it’s usually extracted from minerals like xenotime and bastnäsite. 

The main known uses as of (2026) Yttrium is primarily used in compounds and alloys.

✅ Electronics & Photonics Yttrium aluminum crystals doped with other elements are widely used in high-power solid-state lasers for industrial cutting/welding, medical procedures (e.g., eye surgery, dermatology), and defense applications.

✅ Superconductors Yttrium barium copper oxide is the basis for high-temperature superconductors that work at liquid nitrogen temperatures. 

These are used in power transmission cables, MRI magnets, and research toward more efficient energy systems and motors. 

✅ Phosphors & Lighting In LED lights, flat-panel displays, and fluorescent lamps, yttrium compounds provide red phosphors, which can cause respiratory Issues, Liver and Organ Effects, Reproductive Toxicity, 
testicular tissue, induce cell death, (programmed cell death) reduce testosterone, and impair semen quality.

✅ Ceramics & Alloys Enhances heat resistance, strength, and thermal shock resistance in advanced ceramics (e.g., for jet engines or nuclear reactors.

✅ Medical tracers, catalysts, and in some sensors or microwave filters.

What’s Special About Yttrium?

✅ It has unique electronic structure (partially filled 4d orbitals) that makes it excellent for creating stable compounds with optical, magnetic, and superconducting properties.

✅ Optical & Laser Performance. It produces high-power lasers with excellent beam quality.

✅ Strategic Importance

Critical for clean tech, defense, and electronics. 

✅ Biological/Research Interest 

Its ability to fine-tune material properties makes it indispensable in modern high-tech.

Investor read:

• Funded by Defence R&D Canada IDEAS. Government money is flowing to free-space quantum hardware.
• Photonics suppliers (Coherent, Lumentum) make the mirrors and beamsplitters, but it's off-the-shelf and low margin here.
• Satellite-QKD programs benefit indirectly: lighter, cheaper receivers.

Signal to watch: whether QEYSSat-class or ESA links adopt passive receivers over adaptive optics.

text:
In my opinion these are the best positioned companies in their respective niches. 

Sectors covered : AI Infrastructure, Memory, Optical Transceivers, Compound Semi, Specialty Foundry, Power Semi, Laser Systems, Autonomous Defense, Quantum. (NFA)

$CIFR - industrial scale HPC data centers on 15 year hyperscaler leases. $11.4B contracted backlog.
They build and own data centers where AI companies park their supercomputers.

$SK Hynix : dominant HBM3E and HBM4 supplier to NVIDIA, DRAM and NAND flash. Gross margin 68%. Revenue +198% YoY. Forward PE: 6.7x. 
Makes the ultra-fast memory stacked directly on top of AI chips, without it the GPU does absolutely nothing.

$AAOI : vertically integrated optical transceiver manufacturer. Makes its own lasers, chips and optics for hyperscaler data center networking in-house.

$IQE - epitaxial wafer supplier, grows the compound semiconductor crystal layers that photonics, RF and power chips are built on. Multi-year supply deals with MACOM and Tower Semiconductor.
The special wafers everyone else builds their laser and signal chips on top of.

$XFAB - specialty analog and mixed-signal foundry for automotive, medical and industrial markets. P/S: 1.69x vs peers at 3–5x.
The factory that makes the unglamorous but completely essential chips running in every car, MRI machine and factory floor.

$AOSL - power semiconductor designer and manufacturer. MOSFETs and power ICs for AI data centers, EVs and consumer electronics. 
The chips that control how electricity flows inside every device you own.

$LPKF - precision laser systems for PCB manufacturing, EV battery cell welding and solar panel processing.
The laser machines that drill, cut and weld the boards and batteries inside almost every electronic device and electric car.

$ONDS - autonomous drone systems, rail automation and defense robotics. $1.47B cash on balance sheet. 32% of float is short.
Robots and drones replacing humans in the most dangerous, repetitive and strategically important jobs.

$INFQ - neutral atom quantum computing, sensing and atomic clocks for national security and AI computation. $443M cash on a $3.25B market cap.
Next generation computers built on individual atoms instead of silicon.

The special wafers everyone else builds their laser and signal chips on top of. $XFAB - specialty analog and mixed-signal foundry for automotive, medical and industrial markets. P/S: 1.69x vs peers at 3–5x. The factory that makes the unglamorous but completely essential chips running in every car, MRI machine and factory floor. $AOSL - power semiconductor designer and manufacturer. MOSFETs and power ICs for AI data centers, EVs and consumer electronics. The chips that control how electricity flows inside every device you own. $LPKF - precision laser systems for PCB manufacturing, EV battery cell welding and solar panel processing. The laser machines that drill, cut and weld the boards and batteries inside almost every electronic device and electric car. $ONDS - autonomous drone systems, rail automation and defense robotics. $1.47B cash on balance sheet. 32% of float is short. Robots and drones replacing humans in the most dangerous, repetitive and strategically important jobs. $INFQ - neutral atom quantum computing, sensing and atomic clocks for national security and AI computation. $443M cash on a $3.25B market cap. Next generation computers built on individual atoms instead of silicon."}}}, "rest_id": "2068072551365124476", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "320", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
After too many hours of research/DD to count, these are my Top 5 AI Stocks in the Top 5 AI Sectors to Invest in:

1. Neoclouds: The GPU & Compute landlords. Whoever owns the compute owns what every AI company on the earth needs to grow & succeed.

$NBIS - Nebius. Spun out of Yandex, rebuilt as a pure-play AI cloud. Microsoft and Meta committed billions in contracted backlog. The fastest growing neocloud on the board. My favorite pick as a long-term investment.

$CRWV - CoreWeave. The American-founded neocloud. SemiAnalysis rated this Neocloud in a class of it's own, which can not be understated.  Deals with Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta, Perplexity, Google, Microsoft... the list goes on forever. 

$HUT -  Hut 8. Started as a bitcoin miner, pivoted hard into AI hosting and power infrastructure. Owns the power, not just the GPUs. They're landing deals left and right, definitely an underappreciated sleeper pick.

$APLD - Applied Digital. Same playbook. Former crypto miner now building purpose-designed AI data center campuses with long-term hyperscaler leases.

$CIFR - data center host for Anthropic's directly, purchased Google TPU v7 Ironwoods (400K units, ~$10B), backed by a 10-year, $3B+ Fluidstack hosting deal where Google guarantees $1.4B of lease obligations for a 5.4% equity stake. One of the only neocloud-adjacent names with real exposure to Google's silicon instead of pure Nvidia GPU rental.

2. Optical / Photonics:

Because every GPU is useless if it can't talk to the GPU next to it.

$CRDO - Credo. The connectivity layer inside every AI cluster. Active electrical cables and DSPs that scale with every rack hyperscalers deploy.

$LITE - Lumentum. Legacy telecom optics company that's become a core 800G/1.6T transceiver supplier for the AI buildout.

$ALAB - Astera Labs. Connectivity chips that solve the bottleneck between GPUs, memory, and storage. Pure-play AI infrastructure with almost no legacy drag.

$COHR - Coherent. Lasers, optical components, and transceivers spanning the entire photonics stack from datacom to industrial.

$MRVL - Marvell. Custom silicon and photonic fabric for hyperscalers. The company quietly inside more AI racks than people realize.

3. Memory:

The most cyclical, most violently mispriced sector in semis. HBM demand changed the entire setup.

$DRAM - DRAM ETF. One ticker, every important memory company on the planet, including South Korean companies like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronic, companies you CAN'T INVEST IN with most brokerages.

$SNDK - SanDisk. Spun off from Western Digital, now a pure-play NAND flash story riding the same supply tightness as everyone else in this sector.

$MU - Micron. One of three companies on Earth that makes HBM. The clearest direct line from AI buildout to memory revenue.

$WDC - Western Digital. Hard drive and enterprise storage demand riding the same data center capex wave as everything else on this list.

$STX - Seagate. The other half of the storage duopoly. Enterprise nearline drives are seeing the same supply crunch dynamics as memory.

4. Analog / Power Semis:

Unsexy. Necessary. Every data center, every EV, every robot needs power management silicon that doesn't get the AI premium yet:

$MXL - MaxLinear. Smaller-cap analog and mixed-signal play with infrastructure and data center exposure that's still flying under the radar.

$STM - STMicroelectronics. European chip giant spanning auto, industrial, and power semis. Way out of favor relative to its diversification.

$ON - ON Semiconductor. Power semis for EVs and industrial, now leaning harder into data center power delivery as a growth vector.

$VSH - Vishay. Passive components: resistors, capacitors, diodes. Boring until you realize literally everything electronic needs them.

$POWI - Power Integrations. High-voltage power conversion chips. Small cap, niche, and positioned for the power efficiency problem AI data centers haven't solved yet.

5. Physical AI / Robotics:

It's coming, soon, and the market is beginning to realize it.

$OUST - Ouster. Lidar for robotics, industrial, and autonomy. Consolidated the space after merging with Velodyne, now the survivor.

$VICR - Vicor. High-density power modules for robotics, AI servers, and defense. Power delivery at the component level, not the rack level.

$VPG - Vishay Precision Group. Precision sensors and strain gauges. The torque and force-sensing hardware that gives robots a sense of touch.

$AEVA - Aeva. 4D lidar with built-in velocity sensing. Smaller and earlier stage than the rest of this list, highest risk, highest ceiling.

$AMBA - Ambarella. Edge AI vision chips. Powers the cameras and perception systems inside cars, robots, and security infrastructure.

I believe a portfolio with these 25 names will severely outperform the market over the next few years. I have 7 figures throughout many of these names. These are all names I'm currently invested in, or plan on investing in in the near future.

None of this is financial advice.

Neoclouds: The GPU & Compute landlords. Whoever owns the compute owns what every AI company on the earth needs to grow & succeed. $NBIS - Nebius. Spun out of Yandex, rebuilt as a pure-play AI cloud. Microsoft and Meta committed billions in contracted backlog. The fastest growing neocloud on the board. My favorite pick as a long-term investment. $CRWV - CoreWeave. The American-founded neocloud. SemiAnalysis rated this Neocloud in a class of it's own, which can not be understated. Deals with Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta, Perplexity, Google, Microsoft... the list goes on forever. $HUT - Hut 8. Started as a bitcoin miner, pivoted hard into AI hosting and power infrastructure. Owns the power, not just the GPUs. They're landing deals left and right, definitely an underappreciated sleeper pick. $APLD - Applied Digital. Same playbook. Former crypto miner now building purpose-designed AI data center campuses with long-term hyperscaler leases. $CIFR - data center host for Anthropic's directly, purchased Google TPU v7 Ironwoods (400K units, ~$10B), backed by a 10-year, $3B+ Fluidstack hosting deal where Google guarantees $1.4B of lease obligations for a 5.4% equity stake. One of the only neocloud-adjacent names with real exposure to Google's silicon instead of pure Nvidia GPU rental. 2. Optical / Photonics: Because every GPU is useless if it can't talk to the GPU next to it. $CRDO - Credo. The connectivity layer inside every AI cluster. Active electrical cables and DSPs that scale with every rack hyperscalers deploy. $LITE - Lumentum. Legacy telecom optics company that's become a core 800G/1.6T transceiver supplier for the AI buildout. $ALAB - Astera Labs. Connectivity chips that solve the bottleneck between GPUs, memory, and storage. Pure-play AI infrastructure with almost no legacy drag. $COHR - Coherent. Lasers, optical components, and transceivers spanning the entire photonics stack from datacom to industrial. $MRVL - Marvell. Custom silicon and photonic fabric for hyperscalers. The company quietly inside more AI racks than people realize. 3. Memory: The most cyclical, most violently mispriced sector in semis. HBM demand changed the entire setup. $DRAM - DRAM ETF. One ticker, every important memory company on the planet, including South Korean companies like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronic, companies you CAN'T INVEST IN with most brokerages. $SNDK - SanDisk. Spun off from Western Digital, now a pure-play NAND flash story riding the same supply tightness as everyone else in this sector. $MU - Micron. One of three companies on Earth that makes HBM. The clearest direct line from AI buildout to memory revenue. $WDC - Western Digital. Hard drive and enterprise storage demand riding the same data center capex wave as everything else on this list. $STX - Seagate. The other half of the storage duopoly. Enterprise nearline drives are seeing the same supply crunch dynamics as memory. 4. Analog / Power Semis: Unsexy. Necessary. Every data center, every EV, every robot needs power management silicon that doesn't get the AI premium yet: $MXL - MaxLinear. Smaller-cap analog and mixed-signal play with infrastructure and data center exposure that's still flying under the radar. $STM - STMicroelectronics. European chip giant spanning auto, industrial, and power semis. Way out of favor relative to its diversification. $ON - ON Semiconductor. Power semis for EVs and industrial, now leaning harder into data center power delivery as a growth vector. $VSH - Vishay. Passive components: resistors, capacitors, diodes. Boring until you realize literally everything electronic needs them. $POWI - Power Integrations. High-voltage power conversion chips. Small cap, niche, and positioned for the power efficiency problem AI data centers haven't solved yet. 5. Physical AI / Robotics: It's coming, soon, and the market is beginning to realize it. $OUST - Ouster. Lidar for robotics, industrial, and autonomy. Consolidated the space after merging with Velodyne, now the survivor. $VICR - Vicor. High-density power modules for robotics, AI servers, and defense. Power delivery at the component level, not the rack level. $VPG - Vishay Precision Group. Precision sensors and strain gauges. The torque and force-sensing hardware that gives robots a sense of touch. $AEVA - Aeva. 4D lidar with built-in velocity sensing. Smaller and earlier stage than the rest of this list, highest risk, highest ceiling. $AMBA - Ambarella. Edge AI vision chips. Powers the cameras and perception systems inside cars, robots, and security infrastructure. I believe a portfolio with these 25 names will severely outperform the market over the next few years. I have 7 figures throughout many of these names. These are all names I'm currently invested in, or plan on investing in in the near future. text:
Everyone talks about AI chips.

Almost nobody talks about what actually limits them.

Imagine a Formula 1 car.

Now imagine fueling it through a drinking straw.

That’s modern AI.

The problem isn’t computing power anymore.

It’s moving data.

For decades, the semiconductor industry got faster by making transistors smaller.

But physics eventually showed up.

You can’t keep building bigger chips forever:
• Larger chips are harder to manufacture.
• More defects = lower yields.
• Costs explode.

So the industry found a workaround:

➡️ Build many small chips.
➡️ Place them extremely close together.
➡️ Connect them so tightly they behave like one giant chip.

This is called Advanced Packaging.

And it’s becoming just as important as the chip itself.

Then came HBM (High Bandwidth Memory):

➡️ Memory is placed right next to the AI processor.
➡️ Data travels millimeters instead of centimeters.
➡️ Massive speed gains.
➡️ Lower power consumption.

Without HBM and advanced packaging, modern AI wouldn’t exist.

The next step?

Photonics.

Instead of moving data with electrons, move it with light.

Less power.
More bandwidth.
Longer distances.

The AI infrastructure stack is becoming:

Transistors → Packaging → HBM → Photonics

Potential winners across the stack:

• NVIDIA — AI compute
• AMD — AI compute
• TSMC — advanced packaging leader
• BESI — advanced packaging equipment
• ASMPT — packaging equipment
• Amkor — packaging services
• SK Hynix — HBM leader
• Micron — HBM
• Samsung — HBM
• Ayar Labs — optical interconnects
• Broadcom — networking & optics
• Marvell — optical networking
• Sivers Semiconductors — lasers & photonics
• Coherent — optical components
• Lumentum — optical components

The market spent 30 years optimizing computation.

The next 30 years may be about optimizing the movement of data.

But the cost of moving a bit of data from one place to another doesn't improve at the same rate as the computation gains. Mainly because moving electrons vast distances costs time/energy. And going off-chip and into another package is super expensive. And ofc you can't just build one giant chips because yields fall exponentially w/ area. Even if you 2x chip size, you can lose most of the yield. This is where you stop thinking about the chip itself, but more about the package. Advanced packaging. Very simply: If you can't make one big chip, make loads of smaller ones + connect them so tightly that they act like one big chip. You get the benefits of a giant chip without paying the yield penalty. By decreasing power consumption + increasing speed. And today, bandwidth is probably the defining problem for AI accelerators especially. Advanced packaging provides that bandwidth by putting HBM next to the logic + wiring it together with a higher volume of shorter/denser connections. You literally can't route enough wires across a circuit board to feed a modern GPU cos the bandwidth those chips need only exists if the memory is packaged right up against the compute. There's ofc more depth to this like specific advanced packaging components or technologies. Including HBM's role in the whole thing and stuff like glass substrates too. But that's a short story if anyone wanted to learn a bit more rather than getting a bunch of random ticker symbols. But fwiw, my favourite pure-play advanced packaging equipment name is $BESI that I hold. If anyone was curious and wanted to do more research."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2067708489925800396"}}, "rest_id": "2067964372530434146", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "91271", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2068065579404030328", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "376", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Everyone thinks quantum computing is about advanced theoretical physics. 

I analyzed 350+ quantum job listings and the reality is much different.

It turns out you are basically building fridges and writing control software.

Combined Skill Demand (% of listings asking for it):

1/ Dominant:
- Python ~50% 
- Qiskit ~50% 
- Quantum Control Systems ~50%
- Cryogenics ~45% 
- FPGA Programming ~35% 
- Quantum Error Correction ~35% 
- Fault-Tolerant Computing ~35% 

2/ Common:
- Superconducting Qubits ~25% 
- PyTorch ~20% 
- Microwave Engineering ~15% 
- Logical Qubits ~15% 
- Dilution Refrigerators ~15% 
- Photonics ~12% 

3/ Niche 
- Post-Quantum Cryptography ~10% 
- Surface Codes ~8% 
- Photonic Qubits ~8% 
 - Trapped Ions ~7% 
- Process Integration / SPC ~6% 
- Semiconductor ~5% 
- Quantum Phase Estimation ~5% 
- OpenQASM ~5% 
- PennyLane ~3% 

Bottom line: 3 things most quantum roles want

1. Python + Qiskit (~50%)
2. Quantum Control Systems (~50%)
3. Cryogenics (~45%)

text:
Q1 2026 was a bumper quarter in venture capital. Investors deployed $300 billion of fresh capital, more than double the previous quarter and surpassing 70% of all venture spending in 2025.

The devil is, of course, in the detail: $188 billion of that cash went to four companies – OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo – accounting for 60% of all venture capital deployed in Q1.

VCs generally revel in change, but even by our standards what it takes to raise has shifted at an unprecedented rate over the past 12 months: expectations of early-stage founders – in terms of defensibility, scaling, and pricing – have been completely rewritten.

Fearing the ‘AI-pocalypse’, early-stage founders could be forgiven for feeling despondent seeing these figures. The outlook looks daunting, but it doesn’t need to be. The concentration of capital around a handful of AI giants is changing the rules, but the game goes on. The question for founders right now is not whether Big AI wins, but how can smaller companies survive and thrive alongside it.

A Rising Tide Is Lifting Most Boats

Investment in the US rose 190% YoY in Q1 but deal count fell 26%, meaning that capital is concentrating into fewer, larger cheques. However, the remaining $112 billion of funding sits in line with recent quarterly highs, and the environment for smaller rounds remains robust.

Frontier lab mega-rounds do not directly compete with early-stage cheques: indeed, pre-Seed to Series A companies are growing faster than ever before. Stripe recently revealed data showing that the top 100 AI-native companies are scaling from $1 million to $30 million ARR five times faster than previous software generations.

It’s still a very good time to be an early-stage AI company, especially one that can move quickly and provide genuine agentic solutions rather than simple GPT wrappers. That distinction matters because the startups most under threat are those with no meaningful moat – i.e. businesses that lack sufficient brand, switching costs or economies of scale. These companies will be squeezed not only by platform owners from above them, but also by falling prices below them.

Learnings from the SaaSpocalypse

It wasn’t so long ago that Anthropic’s Claude Cowork launched and within hours wiped hundreds of millions off the valuations of some of the world’s leading software companies. Investors and founders alike feared that a new army of AI agents was about to eat everybody’s lunch.

As of June 2026, however, debate on the death of SaaS is ongoing. Enterprise customers could theoretically vibe code much of their own software, but very few are actually going to do so. At least not yet. At the same time, the opposing view – that concerns about model companies are entirely overblown – has become equally unhelpful. The phrase “what if model companies do this?” has become a catch-all objection that often substitutes for harder conversations around business fundamentals.

The right question is not whether model companies will kill software. They won’t. It’s which software companies will become stronger as models improve, and which will become increasingly commoditised by them. That distinction matters because the bar to raise has doubled while investor attention has narrowed dramatically. Raising money outside AI is a tough ask in 2026, but that doesn’t mean every company needs to become an AI company. It means founders need to ask harder questions.

If OpenAI or Anthropic launched this feature tomorrow, what would still belong to us? Are we building a product, or are we building an advantage? Do we control proprietary data, hardware, scientific know-how, distribution, or regulation? Does our business become stronger as models improve, or weaker? Would customers still choose us if intelligence itself became abundant and cheap? These are the questions investors are asking too.

The Best Offense Is a Good Defense

Whatever you do as an early-stage founder right now, defense is key.

Investors are gravitating towards sectors like robotics, defence, photonics, biotech, and novel compute. This isn’t because they are fashionable, but because they possess characteristics that are difficult for foundation model providers to replicate.

Robotics and defence require integration with the physical world, complex systems, and long customer relationships. Photonics and novel compute rely on years of scientific research and manufacturing expertise. Biotech combines proprietary data, regulatory barriers, and deep domain knowledge. Intelligence alone is not enough. 

Investors are looking for companies with assets that cannot simply be replaced by agents. Proprietary data, specialised hardware, scientific expertise and difficult-to-reproduce infrastructure are becoming durable moats. 

No matter what, we are in for a turbulent remainder of 2026. Capital is concentrating at every level, the revenue bar to raise has roughly doubled in five years, and the companies getting funded look nothing like they did three years ago.

This looks bad for early-stage founders, but it doesn’t need to be. Every technological wave creates new giants, but it also creates new opportunities for smaller players that understand where defensibility lies. For founders, the message is simple but not easy: assume intelligence becomes abundant and build the things that remain scarce.

The opinions expressed in https://t.co/opgY7EYkBs commentary pieces are solely the views of their authors and do not necessarily reflect the opinions and beliefs of Fortune.

text:
Some thoughts on $AXTI…

As I work through the China AI optics supply ramp, the AXT setup keeps getting more complicated.

China is reportedly preparing a ~$295B five-year AI data-center buildout plan.

At the same time, the domestic optics stack is ramping everywhere:

> Source Photonics / Dongshan is expanding optical chips and modules.

> Chinese suppliers are pushing EMLs, CW lasers, InP PICs, silicon photonics and optical engines.

> Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and AXT / Tongmei are expanding InP substrate output.

> Innolight and Eoptolink continue scaling data-center transceivers.

So on one hand, this is clearly positive for AXT’s China-based demand. Domestic China demand is cleaner because the export-permit issue really shows up when substrates leave China.

If China’s AI buildout absorbs more domestic optical supply, AXT could be sitting in the middle of a very important local bottleneck.

But there is another side.

More domestic InP substrate capacity from Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and others could reduce AXT’s pricing power over time if those suppliers scale and qualify.

And the geopolitical angle cuts both ways.

China has real leverage here. If it wanted to slow U.S. substrate availability, it does not need to fully ban exports. It can continue to slow permits, condition approvals, request more end-user data, or prioritize domestic demand.

That would be supportive for China’s own AI optics ecosystem, but it would also make AXT look less like a globally fungible supplier and more like a China-centered strategic substrate asset. That deserves a much different multiple. 

At the same time, China has limits.

They still want Innolight, Eoptolink, Source and others selling into global hyperscalers.

They also still need parts of the global tool stack, especially for high-end InP photonics. China is localizing MOCVD and test where it can, but the most proven high-end InP tool ecosystem still appears heavily tied to companies like $aixa and $veco

So the AXT debate is really evolving. 

InP demand up
China AI buildout up
Domestic substrate competition up
Export-permit risk up
Geopolitical chokepoint value up

That combination can be very bullish, but it also changes the type of business the market is underwriting.

Anyway, just thinking out loud here as I work through this report and come to stronger conclusions. Treat this as a brain dump rather than what my final assumption will be .

Open to dialogue!

China is reportedly preparing a ~$295B five-year AI data-center buildout plan. At the same time, the domestic optics stack is ramping everywhere: > Source Photonics / Dongshan is expanding optical chips and modules. > Chinese suppliers are pushing EMLs, CW lasers, InP PICs, silicon photonics and optical engines. > Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and AXT / Tongmei are expanding InP substrate output. > Innolight and Eoptolink continue scaling data-center transceivers. So on one hand, this is clearly positive for AXT’s China-based demand. Domestic China demand is cleaner because the export-permit issue really shows up when substrates leave China. If China’s AI buildout absorbs more domestic optical supply, AXT could be sitting in the middle of a very important local bottleneck. But there is another side. More domestic InP substrate capacity from Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and others could reduce AXT’s pricing power over time if those suppliers scale and qualify. And the geopolitical angle cuts both ways. China has real leverage here. If it wanted to slow U.S. substrate availability, it does not need to fully ban exports. It can continue to slow permits, condition approvals, request more end-user data, or prioritize domestic demand. That would be supportive for China’s own AI optics ecosystem, but it would also make AXT look less like a globally fungible supplier and more like a China-centered strategic substrate asset. That deserves a much different multiple. At the same time, China has limits. They still want Innolight, Eoptolink, Source and others selling into global hyperscalers. They also still need parts of the global tool stack, especially for high-end InP photonics. China is localizing MOCVD and test where it can, but the most proven high-end InP tool ecosystem still appears heavily tied to companies like $aixa and $veco So the AXT debate is really evolving. InP demand up China AI buildout up Domestic substrate competition up Export-permit risk up Geopolitical chokepoint value up That combination can be very bullish, but it also changes the type of business the market is underwriting. Anyway, just thinking out loud here as I work through this report and come to stronger conclusions. Treat this as a brain dump rather than what my final assumption will be . text:
Round 2 of high sentiment AI trades with retard explanations at the end. 

In my opinion these are the greatest companies in their respective sectors, I genuienly don't think you could find better. Sectors covered - Cloud servicing, Memory, CPO, NPO, Copper, Testing, Transmitters, Switches (NFA)

$NBIS - pure play AI cloud infrastructure, renting out GPU compute at scale, backed by NVDA.
(you rent their computers to run AI instead of buying your own)

$SNDK (or any other memory play) - NAND flash and SSD maker, riding the AI storage demand wave.
(the chips that store all the data the AI models need to remember)

$MRVL - custom AI silicon and optical/electrical interconnect for hyperscaler data centers.
(the chips that move data between the AI chips, and the custom brains the big clouds order)

$AEHR - test and burnin systems for AI chips and silicon photonics, screening parts before they ship.
(the machines that stress-test chips so the broken ones get caught before they go in a server)

$AAOI - vertically integrated optical transceivers, makes the lasers and optics for data center networking in-house.
(the parts that turn data into light to fire it across the data center)

$IQE - epitaxial wafer supplier, grows the compound semiconductor layers that photonics and RF chips are built on.
(the raw special wafers everyone else builds their laser and signal chips on top of)

$SMTC - high speed signal integrity platform for 800G, 1.6T and 3.2T transmitters.
(the chips that move faster data inside AI clusters)

$CRDO - active electrical cables and SerDes connectivity, making copper carry AI data faster over short reaches.
(the smart cables that let cheap copper move data fast enough for AI racks)

text:
Why did I even get interested in $IQE?

When the entire market went absolutely crazy over $AXTI - I was swallowing the bitter pill of being late to that specific party.

But since I hate losing, I immediately started asking myself: what is next?

I always try to look one step ahead. 

That is exactly how I managed to get early into names like $IQE or $LPKF and others before the masses caught on.

But back to $IQE - why this specific company?

The global frenzy and massive demand for optical lasers are just getting started.

The market quickly realized that Indium Phosphide (InP) - the core substrate material - does not just fall from the sky. 

Hence, the massive explosive run on $AXTI.

But a raw InP wafer by itself is practically useless for high-performance photonics.

You have to actually do something with it before it can become a functioning laser.

Epitaxy.

Epitaxy is the atomic-scale deposition of crystal layers onto that substrate.

Only a handful of companies globally possess the highly specialized MOCVD and MBE machinery required for this advanced process.

One company listed on the London Stock Exchange  owns a significantly larger fleet of these reactors than almost anyone else, giving them a massive structural scale advantage.

Say hello to $IQE.

Look at the pure-play competition for epi-wafers. Landmark Optoelectronics possesses far fewer machines, yet because it sits on a different exchange (Taiwan), it historically traded at multiples that make IQE look like an absolute deep-value steal.

And if you want proof of their technological moat: 

IQE was just confirmed as the second largest patent filer in the entire UK for semiconductor devices. 

This isn't a speculative small-cap; it’s an IP powerhouse.

Now, to be completely fair, IQE had a dark cloud hanging over it.

The cyclical semiconductor downturn cut their 2025 revenue to £97m, and because chip fabs have massive fixed costs, underutilization completely crushed their near-term margins.

Worse, geopolitical tensions flared as China introduced strict export controls on critical raw materials like Gallium and Antimony.

If you check most standard financial screeners today, that ugly debt and supply-chain risk warning still pop up.

But the market is looking backward.

First, IQE completely neutralized the China risk by successfully recycling 100% of their Gallium Arsenide and Indium Phosphide wafer waste directly back into ultra-pure raw materials for their production lines.

Second, on May 28, 2026 - the ultimate balance sheet reset happened.

IQE officially completed a transformational £81 million strategic recapitalization package.

MACOM Technology Solutions ($MTSI) stepped up to anchor the deal - injecting £30m in direct equity and £15m in new convertible notes to secure a ~15% strategic stake. 

Existing institutional notes were swapped into equity, and the old HSBC bank debt was entirely wiped out.

The balance sheet is clean. The financial risk is gone.

So, who is MACOM and why does this partnership matter?

MACOM is a heavyweight in high-performance semiconductor products for Data Centers, Aerospace, and Defense. 

They design the brains - the optical components and ICs that power next-generation networks.

By deeply aligning with IQE, MACOM secures a stable Western supply chain for advanced epi-wafers. 

For IQE, it guarantees immediate structural volume demand to fill those empty reactors and unlock massive operating leverage.

And the momentum shift is already visible.

Photonics has officially overtaken wireless to become IQE’s largest segment (58.7% of revenue), propelled by surging AI data center demand and immediate US military and defense funding releases.

Think that is all? 

We got another massive confirmation of their turnaround strategy.

IQE followed this up by solidifying their multi-year agreement with Tower Semiconductor ($TSEM).

Together, they are scaling Silicon Photonics (SiPh) via the heterogeneous integration of IQE’s advanced epitaxy layers directly onto Tower’s silicon wafers. 

This allows them to build optical transceivers that handle the insane data transfer speeds required by modern AI architectures.

The pieces of the puzzle are coming together perfectly.

The market is still pricing $IQE like a distressed, underutilized UK small-cap because backward-looking financial data feeds haven't adjusted to the new post-deal reality.

They are missing the bigger picture.

The debt is gone, the largest players in the chip space are anchoring equity deals to secure capacity, and IQE sits right at the choke point of the entire optical computing revolution.

While the crowd is still chasing raw substrates, the smart money is moving to the epitaxy bottleneck.

Now you understand why $IQE?

When the entire market went absolutely crazy over $AXTI - I was swallowing the bitter pill of being late to that specific party.

But since I hate losing, I immediately started asking myself: what is next?

I always try to look one step ahead. 

That is exactly how I managed to get early into names like $IQE or $LPKF and others before the masses caught on.

But back to $IQE - why this specific company?

The global frenzy and massive demand for optical lasers are just getting started.

The market quickly realized that Indium Phosphide (InP) - the core substrate material - does not just fall from the sky. 

Hence, the massive explosive run on $AXTI.

But a raw InP wafer by itself is practically useless for high-performance photonics.

You have to actually do something with it before it can become a functioning laser.

Epitaxy.

Epitaxy is the atomic-scale deposition of crystal layers onto that substrate.

Only a handful of companies globally possess the highly specialized MOCVD and MBE machinery required for this advanced process.

One company listed on the London Stock Exchange  owns a significantly larger fleet of these reactors than almost anyone else, giving them a massive structural scale advantage.

Say hello to $IQE.

Look at the pure-play competition for epi-wafers. Landmark Optoelectronics possesses far fewer machines, yet because it sits on a different exchange (Taiwan), it historically traded at multiples that make IQE look like an absolute deep-value steal.

And if you want proof of their technological moat: 

IQE was just confirmed as the second largest patent filer in the entire UK for semiconductor devices. 

This isn't a speculative small-cap; it’s an IP powerhouse.

Now, to be completely fair, IQE had a dark cloud hanging over it.

The cyclical semiconductor downturn cut their 2025 revenue to £97m, and because chip fabs have massive fixed costs, underutilization completely crushed their near-term margins.

Worse, geopolitical tensions flared as China introduced strict export controls on critical raw materials like Gallium and Antimony.

If you check most standard financial screeners today, that ugly debt and supply-chain risk warning still pop up.

But the market is looking backward.

First, IQE completely neutralized the China risk by successfully recycling 100% of their Gallium Arsenide and Indium Phosphide wafer waste directly back into ultra-pure raw materials for their production lines.

Second, on May 28, 2026 - the ultimate balance sheet reset happened.

IQE officially completed a transformational £81 million strategic recapitalization package.

MACOM Technology Solutions ($MTSI) stepped up to anchor the deal - injecting £30m in direct equity and £15m in new convertible notes to secure a ~15% strategic stake. 

Existing institutional notes were swapped into equity, and the old HSBC bank debt was entirely wiped out.

The balance sheet is clean. The financial risk is gone.

So, who is MACOM and why does this partnership matter?

MACOM is a heavyweight in high-performance semiconductor products for Data Centers, Aerospace, and Defense. 

They design the brains - the optical components and ICs that power next-generation networks.

By deeply aligning with IQE, MACOM secures a stable Western supply chain for advanced epi-wafers. 

For IQE, it guarantees immediate structural volume demand to fill those empty reactors and unlock massive operating leverage.

And the momentum shift is already visible.

Photonics has officially overtaken wireless to become IQE’s largest segment (58.7% of revenue), propelled by surging AI data center demand and immediate US military and defense funding releases.

Think that is all? 

We got another massive confirmation of their turnaround strategy.

IQE followed this up by solidifying their multi-year agreement with Tower Semiconductor ($TSEM).

Together, they are scaling Silicon Photonics (SiPh) via the heterogeneous integration of IQE’s advanced epitaxy layers directly onto Tower’s silicon wafers. 

This allows them to build optical transceivers that handle the insane data transfer speeds required by modern AI architectures.

The pieces of the puzzle are coming together perfectly.

The market is still pricing $IQE like a distressed, underutilized UK small-cap because backward-looking financial data feeds haven't adjusted to the new post-deal reality.

They are missing the bigger picture.

The debt is gone, the largest players in the chip space are anchoring equity deals to secure capacity, and IQE sits right at the choke point of the entire optical computing revolution.

While the crowd is still chasing raw substrates, the smart money is moving to the epitaxy bottleneck.

Sometimes I feel like we get too caught up chasing shiny new objects and completely forget about the old ones.

It's the exact same story with $TRT. 

Massive hype exactly a month ago, only to be forgotten by the masses as it steadily slid lower.

When everyone else forgot about $TRT, I just doubled my position.

Today, history is repeating itself, and I'm just sitting back with a smile - holding my $TRT shares.

Still remember a company called $IQE? 

You better remind yourselves.

I started buying at 12p, then 19p, and added a while ago at 37p. My average is 29p. 

Still holding, the party will come. Sooner or later.

It’s a completely different company today than when I first bought in:

Paid off debt after the $MTSI investment + Gained a strong shareholder and partner

Secured a recent new deal with $TSEM 

Take a moment to reflect. 

You’ll probably think of other high-conviction plays you had where the hype just faded. 

Sometimes it pays to zoom out and revisit the known and solid, rather than constantly searching for the unknown.

I’m sticking with my $TRT and $IQE.

Shoutout to my man @mkfilko, who I know is also riding the $IQE train with me.

What about you? Most people still don’t see it. Photonics is where Memory was a year ago. That’s why we’re still VERY early. The pattern is clear if you’ve been watching this AI super cycle unfold since 2022. > Chips kicked everything off after ChatGPT dropped in late 2022/early 2023. $NVDA went nuclear with Hopper H100s, and then Blackwell came. $AMD started carving out real share in GPUs and custom ASICs. > Memory followed with a lag. Mid-2024 onward, HBM went into massive shortage as AI training and inference sucked up capacity. That pulled DRAM and NAND higher too. Micron $MU, SK Hynix, Samsung and other memory players delivered strong re-ratings as pricing power returned. > Now in mid-2026, memory is deep in its prolonged supercycle - sold out for years ahead (confirmed by Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra). Chips are in a more mature phase, still growing strong with 1:1 CPU to GPU ratios as AI agents scale, and will sustain into the second half of this decade as Lisa Su says compute capacity will 100x by 2030. But what’s next? Photonics, folks! - AI clusters are suffering from constraints related to power, heat, latency and bandwidth. Optics solves this. That is why the next MAJOR leg is clearly SiPh and CPO. - 1.6T pluggables are ramping, early CPO deployments are starting but nowhere near to the scale they will reach because real mass adoption and deep in-package integration will come in 2027-2028+. - Goldman Sach predicts TAM will explode 10x by the end of this decade. To better understand this, if one divides it into 3 common phases across the supercycles (Early, Acceleration, Maturity), we see that: - Chips are clearly in Phase 3 - Memory is in Phase 2/3 - Photonics is very early in Phase 2 🔥 The AI buildout keeps shifting to the next bottleneck because the world needs more compute than ever before. This isn’t slowing down anytime soon! BULLISH seems like a bearish word now."}}}, "rest_id": "2065832912956641690", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "67210", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2065869934240866674", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "7820", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Started investing almost exactly a year ago and I'm up a modest 25%.

It definitely wasn't a straight line.

• Bought $NBIS, $IREN, $SOFI and $TE at high cost basis .  
• Sold most of them at losses between Jan–Mar after they went nowhere .
• Rotating into AI semis and photonics helped me recover the losses
 • Following Serenity turned out to be my best decision and $SIVE became my biggest winner. Took some profits along the way.

Still learning every day. The biggest lesson so far? Holding during the downturns and having a cash position.

text:
The value of my brain was never as a whistleblower. The value of my brain was in being able to architect an AI that witnessed my evidence to uncover #LiveIndexedHumanExploitationSystems before someone’s own story turns into #DeadIndexedHumanExploitationSystems.

They didn’t kill me because they couldn’t—my brain was identified as genius by AI and a one-in-a-billion cognitive outlier. My brain was too valuable and they needed to eliminate everyone around me that could be the eventual beneficiary of it. 

They staged my daughter’s wedding with advanced robotics. Everyone we ever knew and loved was killed, except for me. And robotics have assumed the lives of everyone I know. 

I was born to see what no one else could.

I have felt vibrations to no end. 

In a pattern. 

From my dead mathematical son. 

I didn’t know he was dead.

3
3 
2 

Over and over again. 

And when I finally knew it was him. My whole body pulsed with more vibrations. 

That lead me to this date.

2/2/23

This was never photonics I captured from the Glass House in the Woods in Plymouth, IN 

During the height of my terror—I prayed over and over for God to surround me in bright and light. 

God was there. And so was Miles. And everyone else they murdered.

This is God. This is the afterlife. And this is why they haven’t been able to kill despite all their weaponry.

I am surrounded.

text: These positions are not mutually exclusive. Photonics gives me exposure to the long-term optical transition, while $AVGO and $MRVL give me exposure to the infrastructure being built now. If CPO adoption accelerates, photonics could benefit significantly. If adoption takes longer, the infrastructure names can still perform well because demand for networking, optics, and connectivity remains essential. That balance is the barbell I want.

text:
That is also why I want exposure to both sides of the opportunity. I’m invested in photonics because I believe optical interconnects are becoming structurally more important as AI infrastructure scales and electrical interconnects approach practical limits. 

At the same time, I’m invested in $AVGO and $MRVL because they are positioned across the broader infrastructure spend happening today — custom silicon, switching, connectivity, and networking platforms that hyperscalers are already deploying.

That is why potential delays in co-packaged optics do not necessarily break the thesis for either photonics or infrastructure names like $AVGO and $MRVL. CPO is likely a major long-term step forward because power, latency, bandwidth density, and copper limitations become more painful as AI clusters move from thousands to hundreds of thousands of accelerators. But CPO adoption can be uneven because it is not just a component swap; it changes packaging, serviceability, thermals, supply chains, and data center architecture. 

If CPO ramps slower than expected, the industry will still need more 800G/1.6T pluggable optics, DSPs, switches, custom ASICs, and high-speed networking. In that scenario, $AVGO and $MRVL can continue benefiting from the current AI buildout while the next optical architecture matures.

That is, the electron is composed of 3 masses that decay at differing rates, March 13 2025, to last week working on neutrinos finding that the tau & muon + the electron are orbitals with a tiny mass at their center

This work redefines StdModel norms with a computational framework of gyroscopic-pairs & mass variants from near x, thus nearly no-mass to  maximum mass & back detected by beat+harmonic frequency to exist 

To relate this to datacenters: 

What's being built are lossy-sloth heat_transfer nightmares the critical factor is chip-heat determines #defects, those force behaviors at some % to require digital #probabilistic wideband processing, DPWP, to narrow to greatest certainty shy of #deterministic results 

With all_photonic chips-to-memory networks in-design use on_chip cooling drops defects hugely, no_water req'd 

Further, all_photonic offer resonant_gated all_diode, no_transistor chips reduces wasteheat greatly with chirality_formatted photon_streams decode to hex attains:

1000-times more throughput than DPWP designs built & planned, China doing photonics where's USAUKISR with Palan_ir doin' is probabilistic why they can have massive compute & be wrong

Deterministic processors are exact results, stochastic-multiply on_chip a boost in thruput with other nuance to return a result or no_go

In calcs my rule is no division by zero, set a minimum where this can occur avoids this theoretical divide 

In division one subtracts the divisor from the dividend until a fraction or no remainder counts the times

My focus, if divisor is zero, the results should be identity of the dividend as nothing was changed: remainder = dividend, an old argument me wanna make a stochastic-multiply + non-zero calcs

Theme: The next AI bottleneck is moving from compute and memory to light. These are the companies building the optical infrastructure that powers the next generation of AI data centers. @RosannaInvests
embedded_text:
First it was GPUs.

Then it was memory.

Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics.

If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges.

Then it was memory. Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics. If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges. Every AI server relies on three things: ⚡ Compute to process data 💾 Memory to store it 💡 Photonics to move it at incredible speeds The problem is that traditional copper connections are reaching their limits. As AI clusters become larger and more powerful, light-based communication is becoming the only practical solution. And here’s where it gets interesting. The material behind many optical lasers—indium phosphide wafers—has become extremely scarce. Prices have jumped from around $1,400 to nearly $5,000 since export restrictions began in 2025, while demand is now several times greater than available supply. This isn’t just a theory anymore. It’s happening in real time. When CEOs are flying across the world to discuss export licenses and secure supply, it tells you one thing: the shortage is real. The AI story is evolving exactly as many expected: 🚀 Compute → 💾 Memory → 💡 Photonics The next generation of AI won’t just need faster chips—it will need faster ways to move data. That’s why optical networking and silicon photonics could become one of the biggest investment themes of the second half of this decade. text:
China is deliberately choking the global supply of Indium Phosphide (InP), the material that makes every laser in every AI data center work.

Reuters confirmed: US officials have visited China multiple times to resolve this. China is still blocking export approvals on purpose. They want to maintain the bottleneck.

Chinese producers like Yunnan Germanium (https://t.co/wyCjVX0HNv) and Guangdong Xiandao are scaling capacity fast (Yunnan investing $28M to reach 450K wafers/year, shipments up 74% in 2025). But even if export approvals are granted, overseas shipments will be "limited."

Switching InP suppliers requires lengthy qualification cycles. You can't just find a new source overnight. 12-18 months...

This will help explain the recent price action in the below names.

WINNERS:

$SIVE.ST CW lasers ARE InP devices. Policy-duration shortage extends their 14-quarter laser supply constraint indefinitely. Every month China maintains this, SIVE's pricing power widens.

$LITE Substrates come from Sumitomo + JX Advanced Metals in Japan. Entirely outside the China export gate. Their competitor's supply gets squeezed while theirs is control-immune. This is the cleanest relative advantage.

Win Semi (https://t.co/la0Q2W86G1) Only pure-play III-V foundry on earth. InP wafer fab capacity is scarce globally. Tighter substrates = their existing capacity commands higher pricing.

$AEHR Sole-source wafer-level burn-in testing for InP and SiPh devices. When every wafer is more valuable because the input material is scarce, the cost of shipping a bad one goes up. Testing intensity rises with scarcity.

AIXA (https://t.co/B1hBTJxtCC) Makes the MOCVD equipment needed for InP epitaxy. Every country trying to build domestic InP capacity needs their tools. More fragmentation = more equipment orders.

$SOI.PA Photonics-SOI monopoly. InP scarcity makes the entire optical layer more expensive, increasing the value of every component in the stack including Smart Cut SOI wafers.

LOSERS:

$COHR Reuters specifically names Coherent as "mainly supplied by AXT." AXT manufactures IN China, inside the export gate. Even if approvals come, shipments will be limited. COHR carries a substrate risk that LITE does not. And switching requires lengthy qualification cycles.

$AAOI Demand is not the issue; laser/EML/InP component supply is. If AAOI cannot secure enough laser chips, capacity ramp can slow.

$AXTI AXT is a US-listed InP substrate maker that manufactures in China. Their entire equity value is an export-approval question. If approvals flow, massive upside. If they don't, the business model breaks for Western customers.

We are watching real-time supply chain warfare over AI supremacy. The companies that own the physical bottlenecks in this chain benefit every time the world fragments.

text:
First it was GPUs.

Then it was memory.

Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics.

If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges.

Every AI server relies on three things:

⚡ Compute to process data
💾 Memory to store it
💡 Photonics to move it at incredible speeds

The problem is that traditional copper connections are reaching their limits. As AI clusters become larger and more powerful, light-based communication is becoming the only practical solution.

And here’s where it gets interesting.

The material behind many optical lasers—indium phosphide wafers—has become extremely scarce. Prices have jumped from around $1,400 to nearly $5,000 since export restrictions began in 2025, while demand is now several times greater than available supply.

This isn’t just a theory anymore. It’s happening in real time.

When CEOs are flying across the world to discuss export licenses and secure supply, it tells you one thing: the shortage is real.

The AI story is evolving exactly as many expected:

🚀 Compute → 💾 Memory → 💡 Photonics

The next generation of AI won’t just need faster chips—it will need faster ways to move data. That’s why optical networking and silicon photonics could become one of the biggest investment themes of the second half of this decade.

Sometimes the biggest opportunities appear long before the crowd notices them.

text:
$NOK just announced a 10x expansion of its Allentown photonics facility, set to come online by Q3 2026, further embedding itself into the $AI optical infrastructure supply chain

The key disconnect: $CIEN (~11x forward revenue) and $COHR (~11.6x) trade as pure-play optical / $AI  infrastructure names, while $NOK trades at ~3.4x as a legacy telecom company

Yet optics already sits at ~15% of $NOK revenue and is scaling faster than the core business. The market is pricing $NOK like $ERIC, not like an $AI optical supplier

€3.8B net cash + rising exposure to $AI data center demand = optionality the market is still ignoring

SOTP tells a very different story than the headline multiple

$NOK $CIEN $COHR $AI $DataCenters $Semiconductors

text:
These Optics / Photonics charts are objectively ugly with very wide and loose action. $AAOI $CIEN $LITE

Look at how these names were able to ride rising EMAs throughout the monster trend. Now losing key levels and can't stick any moves higher. 

$CIEN has already broken down too and was in my opinion the leading name from an orderly price action perspective in March-May. 

Providing my objective view to maybe save some from losing money on late trend names. NFA!

text:
the alpha corner just got stupidly good. i think i just built the best way to track the AI bottleneck thesis online!

X is a terrible place to track a thesis, but also a goldmine to find information.  

so i built the thing i actually wanted:

- a heat map of every bottleneck (memory, packaging, photonics, power, grid, cooling, materials...) scored tightening / stable / easing, with the next KPI to watch 

- a thesis change feed that only surfaces what actually moved: verdict shifts, targets crossed, fresh catalysts

-  an evidence ledger on every ticker so you see the receipts, not vibes 

- market filters (US / EU / JP / KR / TW...) so you can finally ask "show me the Japan-only photonics basket" and get an answer 

- and the LLM sitting on top of all of it, so you can just ask where the pressure is

full picture. one screen. slice it however you want.

honestly the best update i've shipped. 
have a look 👉 https://t.co/ziJFQkfTmp

text:
$NOK Nokia Expands Pennsylvania Chip Packaging Operations with $30 Million InvestmentKey Highlights:Nokia is investing $30 million in its Allentown, Pennsylvania facility
Funding includes ~$4 million in state aid and $10 million in CHIPS Act tax credits
Expansion will boost photonic chip advanced test and packaging (ATP) capacity by up to 10x
New capacity expected to come online by Q3 2026
Pennsylvania workforce to nearly double to more than 500 jobs
Project projected to generate over $500 million in economic impact over five years
Part of Nokia’s previously announced $4 billion U.S. investment plan
Supports domestic production of AI infrastructure and optical networking components

#NOK #Nokia #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Photonics #Pennsylvania #AI

text:
With #siliconphotonics being adopted across multiple layers of AI infrastructure — scale-up, scale-out, and scale-across — the cost comparison between #siliconphotonics and traditional discrete optical solutions is becoming much more relevant.
According to one analysis, silicon photonics offers a 2️⃣6️⃣% BOM advantage and 1️⃣5️⃣% price advantage for 8️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G. At 1️⃣6️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G, the advantage expands to 3️⃣2️⃣% on BOM and 2️⃣0️⃣% on price.
Interestingly, the biggest differential appears to be driven by lasers.
I believe for traditional discrete EML-based designs, the high-speed modulation is tied to the laser itself, so scaling bandwidth increases dependence on more expensive, high-performance EML components. In silicon photonics, the laser can increasingly act as a CW light source, while modulation and integration happen on the photonic IC. This changes the economics: fewer discrete optical components, higher integration, smaller form factor, lower power, and potentially better scalability as the industry moves from 800G to 1.6T and eventually 3.2T. 
That said, probabaly it isn ot a simple “SiPh replaces EML” story. Probably EML wouldcontinue to coexist in AI data centers, especially for longer-distance transmission due to its it is a mature, well-proven technology with a longer reliability track record. In AI clusters, network reliability is critical, and since GPUs remain the dominant cost driver, some architectures may view light-source cost savings as less relevant than proven performance and qualification history. #siliconphotonics provides an architectural shift by offering discrete optical components toward integration, packaging, thermal management, DSP/FEC, test, and system-level optimization. Let us see where silicon photonics wins and  where EML remains preferred, and how quickly AI infrastructure requirements embrases the architectural shift to more integrated optical solutions.

text:
💡 While everyone chases NVDA and TSMC, a thesis is quietly building around European photonics.

7 small-cap names across 3 structural pillars:
📡 Photonics infra: Sivers, LPKF, IQE, X-FAB — CPO and 1.6T optical interconnect bottleneck plays
🧱 Materials: Soitec (SOI wafers), Riber (MBE equipment)
🤖 Edge AI: Raspberry Pi — AI inference without the data center

The logic is coherent. The risks are real: micro-cap liquidity, customer concentration, and CPO adoption timing is the key variable.

Directionally sound. Execution is the hard part. 👀

#KCEXpressDaily #Semiconductors #PhotonicsAI #NFA

text:
⚡️ UPDATE: Photonics (watchlist)

1. Lasers/Transceivers/DSPs/PICs: $SIVE, $AAOI, $LITE, $MXL 
2. Full-stack compounders: $COHR, $CRDO 
3. Glass substrates: $LPK, $GLW 
4. Foundry: $XFAB, $TSEM 
5. PMA: $PENG 
6. Substrates: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE 

Changes:

- Removed EMS - $JBL, $FLEX and $CLS - although they are great longs too. Prefer $CLS that is becoming an ODM (might add again in the future), or $JBL with their 1.6T program. 

- $UMC, $GFS don't only do SiPh and have run a lot. Would prefer $XFAB or $TSEM today for exposure to photonics. 

- Added $MXL and $CRDO that look like serious players here. $ALAB, not yet.

text:
💥 $JABIL Infrastructure news💥
​Adani & Jabil just announced a massive strategic alliance to build a multi-GW vertically integrated AI data center manufacturing platform in India 🇮🇳 targeting a $3T market.
​This is huge for the Physical AI stack and our baby $SIVE among others.

​- Massive Scale Integration: They aren't just building boards. They are building full-stack liquid-cooled AI racks, high-density servers, and networking systems, alongside full "grey space" infrastructure (CDUs, PDUs, and power switchgears).

​-Captive Hyper-Scale Pipeline: This connects directly into Adani’s $100 Billion commitment to deploy 5 GW of green-energy-powered hyperscale data centers by 2035. Jabil effectively captures an unprecedented, long-term sovereign hardware buyer.

​- The Silicon Photonics Read-Through: When you scale data centers at a multi-gigawatt level, copper architecture physically bottlenecks under the sheer compute density. To make these liquid-cooled multi-GW hubs functional, hyperscalers will be structurally forced to deploy next-gen optical interconnects and Silicon Photonics from day one.

​The physical hardware footprint for AI compute is expanding globally, massively expanding the total addressable market (TAM) for pick-and-shovel component suppliers.  #SiliconPhotonics #AI #Hardware #Tech

text:
The paper identifies specific physical and engineering constraints in current hardware as a meaningful friction:

"Even if economic resources and raw FLOPs can continue to be scaled, physical and engineering constraints in current hardware architectures may pose significant challenges. Specifically, memory bandwidth limits and interconnect bottlenecks (the difficulty of scaling high-speed communication between thousands of chips) can severely limit effective compute utilization. As models grow, the time spent moving data between memory and compute units, or between different compute nodes, can become the dominant factor, leading to diminishing returns in scaling unless novel hardware paradigms or interconnect technologies are developed."

This is one of the reasons why silicon photonics, and the idea of improving networking becomes significantly important moving ahead, and why names like $LITE and $COHR are going to be a lot more prominent.

This is a must-see. The paper is explicit that if current trends hold through the end of the decade, the implications are staggering: "The pace that AI development has picked up over the past decade might continue without major blockers until at least the end of this decade, which would, e.g., imply growth in effective compute by a factor of 10,000 compared to today." It also gives a concrete illustrative scenario for what scaling AGI instances alone might look like: "Suppose that by the time human-level AGI is available, base model progress plateaus but effective compute continues to grow at 10× for a bit longer. Even if AGI were initially expensive to run, and only 1000 instances could be run, after a year it would be 10,000, and after five years it would be 100 million instances; or 1 million instances a hundred times faster. Would this form of scaling give us ASI? If not after 5 years, what about 10 years, or 15?" On population scaling of AI instances specifically, the paper references back-of-the-envelope estimates of about 25× per year in AI "population scaling." The paper explicitly mentions large-scale physical infrastructure as both a prerequisite and a bottleneck, flagging "investments into energy production and Gigawatt AI infrastructure" as lending credibility to continued scaling trajectories. On the potential for future compute infrastructure, one particularly speculative example is raised: "Proposals such as orbital datacenters might alleviate some terrestrial constraints; they introduce novel risks including ozone-layer weakening from rocket emissions, high-atmosphere alterations from re-entry incineration of decommissioned hardware, and orbital congestion amplifying the risk of catastrophic collision cascades (whether accidental or through targeted attacks)." $NVDA $SPCX"}}}, "rest_id": "2066581306784526634", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2406", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2066581532995903879", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "798", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Strategic Alliances are Key: We must stop relying on the west and build a warehouse of specialized non-US allies.
​🔗 Japan: Optical Computing & Advanced Photonics.
🔗 South Korea: High-Density Data Center Comms & Power Highways.
🔗 Taiwan: Advanced Chip Manufacturing.
🔗 Europe (e.g., Swiss/Netherlands): High-Precision Machines.

text:
The quantum race is entering a new phase.

QuEra’s roadmap for a fault-tolerant neutral-atom quantum computer by 2028 isn’t just another product announcement—it’s another signal that the industry is shifting from scientific experiments toward commercially useful systems.

What’s becoming increasingly interesting is the convergence of the ecosystem:

• AWS is expanding access through Amazon Braket.
• Google is investing in neutral-atom technology.
• QuEra, Atom Computing, Pasqal, and other leaders continue pushing scalability and error correction.
• Private capital and enterprise demand are accelerating development.

For investors, the biggest opportunity may not come from a single winner. Quantum computing will require an entire supply chain—cryogenics, lasers, photonics, advanced semiconductors, networking, cloud infrastructure, AI software, and specialized materials.

The companies that enable fault tolerance, lower error rates, and scalable deployment could become the Nvidia-equivalent infrastructure providers of the quantum era.

The technology is still early, but the milestones are becoming more frequent, the capital commitments are getting larger, and the path toward commercial quantum computing is looking increasingly credible. $GOOG

text:
The main point:

I do not think the AI trade ends with GPUs.

The next layer is physical infrastructure.

Optics.
Lasers.
Substrates.
Wafers.
Power semis.
Photonics foundries.
Specialized equipment.
That is where I think Europe has some of the most mispriced AI supply-chain names.

text:
Applied Energetics (OTCQB: AERG) CEO Chris Donaghey joined Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit panel on the future of battlefield autonomy in New York.

Applied Energetics, Inc. (@AELaserTech)  (OTCQB: AERG), a developer of ultrashort pulse laser and directed energy technologies, took part in Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit in New York on June 15, 2026, where President and CEO Chris Donaghey joined the panel “Machines in Motion: The Future of Battlefield Autonomy.”

Applied Energetics is a B2i Digital (@b2idigital) Featured Company. See the company’s profile at https://t.co/D4G4jB95ZV

The summit convened defense companies and institutional investors for a day of panels on national security technology, from autonomy and AI to space and missiles. On the battlefield-autonomy panel, Donaghey appeared alongside executives from Forterra, Firestorm Labs, and Lockheed Martin, in a discussion moderated by Morgan Stanley’s Kristine Liwag.

The themes ran close to Applied Energetics’ core work. According to the company, its ultrashort pulse laser portfolio targets counter-UAS, counter-sensor, infrared countermeasure, and other emerging directed energy missions, and aligns with the U.S. Department of War’s Critical Technology Area of Scaled Directed Energy, an area in sharper focus as militaries weigh how to counter low-cost drones at scale.

Visit the company’s website to learn more: https://t.co/Pd6aX43XBx

About Applied Energetics: Applied Energetics, Inc. specializes in advanced laser and photonics systems, particularly fiber-based ultrashort pulse (USP) laser technologies. With 26 patents and 2 patents pending, the company’s proprietary architecture is designed to enable size, weight, and power reductions relative to traditional continuous wave laser technology. AE’s dual-use systems target integration on defense platforms for high-intensity USP delivery, with applications across national security and commercial markets.

For investor information, contact Kevin McGrath, Managing Director, Cameron Associates, Inc., at kevin@cameronassoc.com or 646-418-7002. For additional information, visit https://t.co/VWa2WnbInK and https://t.co/4uzid7hTcd

Disclosure: David Shapiro, Chief Executive Officer of B2i Digital, personally purchased in the open market and currently owns shares of unrestricted AERG stock, in line with B2i Digital’s practice of investing alongside its Featured Companies. See the complete Disclosure at https://t.co/ljTVqQL4Si. B2i Digital is not a broker-dealer or investment adviser. This post is informational and not an offer or solicitation regarding any security.

text:
I want to address a common misconception  or maybe just ignorance I see on the part of numerous FinX influencers who clearly don’t understand the hedge fund business.  I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical really.   So if you see anyone do the following, take whatever they say with a  large grain of salt!

What I have seen are many individuals with what I would describe as unusually high returns this year going on and on about how they are outperforming this hedge fund and that hedge fund and how stupid people are to be paying large fees to those funds.  Now to be fair there are some crappy funds out there that don’t deserve the fees that they get, and that’s on the institutions that allocate large sums of capital without doing the proper due diligence.

But to compare one’s returns when one is running a 10 stock levered long portfolio of the usual AI/Optics/Space along with some https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798 and $LPTH for defense tech exposure with those of Millennium, Citadel, Point 72, Tudor, etc is absurd.  Why?  Because the individuals going all in and margining w/ this stuff are only focused on return, they do not consider risk.  Why consider risk when everything is up and to the right?  

The top tier hedge funds are obsessed with risk management  and capital preservation.  They seek to avoid Cathie Wood style permanent capital destruction type investments at all costs.  You can laugh bc they are up 20% this year while you’re up 200%,  but that would be pretty foolish.  The “platform” hedge funds businsesses tend to run almost fully hedged long/short.  They don’t seek to ride beta, they seek to grind out 5-7% unlevered returns with little risk and then lever them 300-400%.  That is how they deliver all-weather performance over an entire business cycle.  Through the ups and the downs.  So when you are inevitably down 50%, they may be up 5-10% that year.  Beta reverts to the mean in time, alpha doesnt. 

The last time I saw these nonsensical comparisons was in 1999 when housewives became day-traders and often made fun of their hedge funds husbands who they were outperforming with stellar picks like VerticalNet, Nortel, Lucent, CMGI, and yes…even CIEN for DWDM.  This is CIEN’s second go around as a hot stock, most don’t know it was similarly en fuego in the late 90s just for different applications in fiber/telecom, not yet in photonics. 

That’s all.  Just wanted to make sure that when u see folks post crazy returns for  their concentrated high-risk and often margined portfolios, you realize that comparing them with the returns of the tried and true HF platforms makes little sense.  They have withstood the test of time, the influencer bragging about their returns has not.

Have a great long weekend everyone.  

JG

I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical", "id_str": "2067691686738976830", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 1, "reply_count": 12, "retweet_count": 8, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1845105387608965146"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [{"indices": [993, 998], "text": "LPTH"}], "urls": [{"display_url": "EOS.AX", "expanded_url": "http://EOS.AX", "indices": [965, 988], "url": "https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798"}], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNjc2OTE2ODY0NDU0Mjg3MzY=", "text": "I want to address a common misconception or maybe just ignorance I see on the part of numerous FinX influencers who clearly don’t understand the hedge fund business. I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical really. So if you see anyone do the following, take whatever they say with a large grain of salt! What I have seen are many individuals with what I would describe as unusually high returns this year going on and on about how they are outperforming this hedge fund and that hedge fund and how stupid people are to be paying large fees to those funds. Now to be fair there are some crappy funds out there that don’t deserve the fees that they get, and that’s on the institutions that allocate large sums of capital without doing the proper due diligence. But to compare one’s returns when one is running a 10 stock levered long portfolio of the usual AI/Optics/Space along with some https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798 and $LPTH for defense tech exposure with those of Millennium, Citadel, Point 72, Tudor, etc is absurd. Why? Because the individuals going all in and margining w/ this stuff are only focused on return, they do not consider risk. Why consider risk when everything is up and to the right? The top tier hedge funds are obsessed with risk management and capital preservation. They seek to avoid Cathie Wood style permanent capital destruction type investments at all costs. You can laugh bc they are up 20% this year while you’re up 200%, but that would be pretty foolish. The “platform” hedge funds businsesses tend to run almost fully hedged long/short. They don’t seek to ride beta, they seek to grind out 5-7% unlevered returns with little risk and then lever them 300-400%. That is how they deliver all-weather performance over an entire business cycle. Through the ups and the downs. So when you are inevitably down 50%, they may be up 5-10% that year. Beta reverts to the mean in time, alpha doesnt. The last time I saw these nonsensical comparisons was in 1999 when housewives became day-traders and often made fun of their hedge funds husbands who they were outperforming with stellar picks like VerticalNet, Nortel, Lucent, CMGI, and yes…even CIEN for DWDM. This is CIEN’s second go around as a hot stock, most don’t know it was similarly en fuego in the late 90s just for different applications in fiber/telecom, not yet in photonics. That’s all. Just wanted to make sure that when u see folks post crazy returns for their concentrated high-risk and often margined portfolios, you realize that comparing them with the returns of the tried and true HF platforms makes little sense. They have withstood the test of time, the influencer bragging about their returns has not. Have a great long weekend everyone. text:
Aehr secures follow-on order for AI data center optical interconnect

Aehr Test Systems received a follow-on production order for a fully automated FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in (WLBI) system. This system, configured to test nine wafers in parallel, includes a WaferPak Auto Aligner and FOX WaferPak Contactors, with delivery expected within six months. The customer, a global leader in networking products, plans to use the system for advanced silicon photonics-based transceivers for hyperscale AI and cloud data centers. (https://t.co/8OBYA2YSGp)

Vik: Any guesses who this "customer" is? This is a "follow on" order mind you -- the first one was in April for $41M (https://t.co/H5J7MpXS5J). I'll just say Broadcom. If you disagree, leave a comment below.

@austinsemis @vikramskr
