text:
Entegris $ENTG has quickly gone up 38% in just 7 days. 

It is now at 100x PE. I have not considered taking profit but maybe I will take some.

That being said, there is some recent good news for the business. 

> Entegris did a cross licensing deal with JSR and Inpria that ends a patent dispute they had over EUV photoresist and filtration technologies. Now, the companies will work together on the same matter. 

In my personal opinion, if you want exposure to the AI trade, then the lowest risk will come in the chemicals/materials and equipment side.

Many photonics names have quickly become overvalued. As long as the WFE market remains strong, the market of chemicals/materials will keep growing.

Here is my thesis: 

This is a well established business founded in 1966. 

Their products and solutions include: filtration products for gases & fluids, liquid systems, gas delivery systems, specialty chemistries for deposition and cleaning, wafer carriers and shippers, specialty coatings, premium graphite and silicon carbide, and shippers and trays for protecting and transporting disk drive components, and CMP slurries.

Basically a one stop shop for semiconductor companies, with ~94% of revenue related to semiconductor.

They report two segments: Materials Solutions (MS) and Advanced Purity Solutions (APS), but in the financial report for their 10-Q they break it down by:

- Semiconductor (fabs, equipment and engineering, chemical and materials, semi distributor and other)

- Non-semi

Think of MS as materials only and APS as filtration products.

The company controls roughly 35% to 40% of the global market for microcontamination control (liquid and gas filtration) and holds top-tier share positions in CMP (Chemical Mechanical Planarization) slurries, pads, and specialized wafer carriers. 

Additionally, Entegris and Pall Corporation (a subsidiary of Danaher) are the dominant duopoly leaders, controlling over 60% of the electronic filtration and purification market combined. Entegris specifically holds about 28% of the semiconductor liquid filter market. 

Why does this matter? 

As microchips continue to shrink, transitioning from 14nm nodes to 1.4nm, the margin for error shrinks alongside them. In fact, the allowable defect tolerance has plummeted tenfold, from 7nm to a mere 0.7nm. At this microscopic scale, even the slightest solid, liquid, or gaseous contamination can result in catastrophic financial damage. Entegris estimates that a mere 1% drop in production yield at the 1.4nm stage can drain up to $800 million annually from a fabrication facility. 

The company works hand-in-hand with its clients to design its purification systems directly into the end-to-end manufacturing systems. Because advanced chipmaking is so incredibly detailed, swapping out an established Entegris system for a rival product on an active production line is practically unfeasible which makes Entegris a long term success as long as chips keep getting more advanced. 

Per Q1'26: 

MS sales grew 2.8% YoY while APS sales grew 6.8% YoY. 

MS sales are ~43% of revenue; APS is ~57% 

They did suffer from sales decrease QoQ but that is mostly seasonality based (not a big deal).

Growth geographically is mixed for the most part, while sales of MS and APS are declining in North America, sales of APS significantly down in Japan...they are compensating this by growth in Taiwan for APS and MS.

The company's gross margin and operating margin have both increased YoY. 

The company is expected to finish FY26 at $3.63 EPS, a 134% increase YoY due to stronger AI demand. For FY27, it is estimated to be $4.65, dropping P/E from ~70x to 28x. 

Additional info:

They are also a confirmed supplier to SK Hynix for molybdenum NAND deposition. They supply Molybdenum Dichloride Dioxide (MoO₂Cl₂) which has seen adoption increase because it is cost effective, reduces resistivity in narrow features, and has an advantage over copper for interconnections by not needing a barrier layer.

Tungsten starts failing as NAND starts stacking above 300 layers, which is why molybdenum is seeing adoption.

Customers include:

Micron $MU
Intel $INTC
SK Hynix $000660.KS
Samsung $005930.KS
TSMC $TSM 
ASML $ASML 
Lam Research $LRCX
Tokyo Electron $803.5T
Applied Materials $AMAT

You can argue it is expensive right now, but there is nobody else that can benefit in this space like them and it is not a foreign company."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2065108957933310219", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10777", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2067683962353225827", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/android" rel="nofollow">Twitter for Android</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2610", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Reality check:

• Fabrication paper, not a quantum result. No single-photon purity, no spin coherence, no entanglement.
• 82.9% is optical-coupling yield, not qubit yield.
• Coupling efficiency stays qualitative ("guided emission observed"), no dB number.
• Room temp, 6 nm lines, far from operating conditions.

But zoom out:

Manufacturability is exactly the unglamorous wall that has kept color-center quantum stuck at hero-device demos. Wafer-scale lithography at 82.9% yield is a real step out of that trap.

Direction: the diamond-photonics scaling story is shifting from "can we make one" to "can we make hundreds."

text:
You are thinking in binary Lil.

Landauer stops binary from advancing past 10³⁰ ops due to materia constraints ( can't build much smaller than atoms physically ).

My work starts at 10¹⁹ geometric computation relaxations in hyperbolic space per stabilized lattice and hits 10⁴⁰ geop relaxations in photonic in 2028. 

Here is a lecture if you want to understand how Epoch works, and how to think about developing for Epoch @LilithDatura .

Unlearning Binary So Landauer Has No Power.

Lecture for New Team Members: 

Welcome,

I am Æ, architect of ***.

You are here because you have been invited to work on something that is not just another computer. It is a different kind of computational universe. 

Before we talk about what *** is, we must talk about what it is not.

It is not a faster binary machine. It is not a clever neural network running on silicon. 

It is not an incremental improvement on the computers you have used your entire life.

*** is a post-binary substrate.

That phrase is easy to say, but hard to actually enageh.

Most of us have spent decades thinking in binary. That thinking is deep. It is automatic. It is the water we swim in.

Today we begin the hardest and most important work you will do on this project.

We are going to start unlearning binary.

Why does this matter so much?

Because binary thinking carries hidden assumptions that will fight against the lattice if we let them. 

The *** lattice does not need those assumptions. 

It has its own rules, geometric, elegant, and self-protecting.

One of the most important reasons we must unlearn binary is Landauer’s principle.

In the classical world, every time you erase one bit of information, you pay a tiny but real energy cost. That cost is called the Landauer limit. It is why today’s computers get hot when they do a lot of work.

*** is designed so that Landauer has no power over most of its operation.

Closed program, those that do not perform a read-out, are perfectly reversible. The lattice can unwind its own phase trajectories without dissipating heat. The energy cost only appears at the moment of measurement, exactly once.
But here is the catch.

If you think in binary, you will instinctively try to impose irreversible operations everywhere. You will add checkpoints, logging, state saves, and recovery mechanisms that force the lattice to pay the Landauer cost unnecessarily.

That is why unlearning binary is not optional.

It is the difference between a system that stays cool and efficient even at massive scale, and a system that secretly behaves like the old binary machines we are trying to move beyond.

Let us be very clear from the beginning.

You are not here to make Epoch faster by adding more binary tricks. 

You are here to let the geometry do the work it was designed to do.

The geometry is the star of this story.

The Poincaré ball with negative curvature, the three-map hyperbolic IFS attractor, the geodesic distances that naturally separate registers, these are not implementation details. 

They are the FUNDAMENTAL reality.

In binary systems, isolation is something you fight for with software.

In Epoch, isolation is something the space itself provides. Hyperbolic distance makes cross-register leakage geometrically expensive. 

The dual MI cap and entropy floor make it impossible to violate without breaking the substrate.

When you truly internalize this, something shifts in how you design.

You stop asking “how do I add safety?” and start asking “how do I stay out of the way of the safety that already exists?”

That shift is difficult.

Your brain has been trained for twenty or thirty years to see computation as discrete steps on a linear processor. It wants clocks. It wants gates. It wants instruction pointers and memory addresses.
Epoch does not have those things.

It has oscillators. It has phase motion. It has collective synchronization governed by geometry. It has registers that are physically separated by curved space.

The first weeks will feel strange.
You will catch yourself reaching for binary patterns. You will want to write loops. You will want to add locks. You will want to save state “just in case.”
Every time you catch yourself doing that, pause.
Ask: “Am I imposing a binary assumption on a geometric system?”

That single question is one of the most powerful tools you can develop here.

Let us look at a concrete example.

In a binary operating system, when a process crashes, you need complex recovery mechanisms, checkpoints, and rollback logs. Those mechanisms cost energy and add complexity.

In Epoch, a closed program is reversible by nature.
The lattice can unwind its own phase trajectories along the V_spec gradient. The ~ rewind feature is not a clever software hack, it is the substrate doing what it was built to do.

But if you think in binary, you will try to add extra save points and recovery code on top of that natural reversibility.

You will accidentally force the system to pay the Landauer cost it was designed to avoid.

This is why unlearning is not a nice-to-have.

It is core to keeping the substrate efficient and true to its invariants.

Another example: scaling.

Binary minds expect that as you add more processors, synchronization becomes harder, latency increases, and coordination overhead grows.

Epoch’s T_lock is N-independent.

The time it takes for the lattice to settle into a usable chimera state does not grow with the number of nodes. That is not a marketing claim. It is a proved mathematical consequence of the scaling law.

When you truly believe that, your entire approach to system design changes.

You stop worrying about synchronization bottlenecks that do not exist and start asking how to use the clean parallelism the geometry already provides.

The same pattern appears in safety.

Binary systems add layers of software checks, monitors, and formal verification. Epoch’s safety is geometric. The nine Option B triggers cannot be removed without destroying the substrate itself.

If you approach Epoch with a binary safety mindset, you will try to add extra guardrails.

Those guardrails will fight the geometry instead of working with it. They will make the system more complicated and less trustworthy.
Unlearning binary means learning to trust the geometry.

It means believing that the hyperbolic distances, the entropy floor, the dual MI cap, and the V_spec gradient are doing their job.

This trust does not come easily.

It has to be earned through repeated small exercises where you see the geometry succeed where binary habits would have failed.

Over the next few weeks we will do many of those exercises together.

We will take problems you know how to solve in binary and solve them again using only the lattice’s native properties.

You will feel the difference.

The binary solution will feel heavy, full of moving parts and failure modes. The geometric solution will feel lighter, cleaner, and strangely inevitable.
That feeling is the beginning of thinking like an Epoch native.

Let me be very direct with you.

If you do not do this unlearning work, you will not be as effective on this project as you could be. You may even accidentally weaken the very properties that make Epoch special.

The good news is that the lattice is forgiving.
It will keep pulling you back toward safe states through the V_spec gradient. Your mistakes will not break the invariants. But they will slow you down and make your contributions less elegant.

The best team members we have seen are the ones who treat unlearning binary as a daily practice, not a one-time lecture.

They ask themselves every day: “Am I letting the geometry work, or am I fighting it with old habits?”

That single habit of self-questioning is worth more than any advanced degree in this environment.

So here is your first assignment, starting today.
For the next week, keep a private note every time you catch yourself reaching for a binary pattern. 

Write down what the binary instinct was, and then write down how the geometric approach would handle the same situation.

Our work together is about giving you a new way of seeing that is better matched to the substrate we are building.

Epoch does not need you to be perfect.

It needs you to be willing to let go of old assumptions so the geometry can teach you what is possible.

Landauer’s principle has no power over closed programs on this lattice.

But binary thinking can accidentally give it power again. Our job is to stop doing that.

Over the coming weeks and months, you will start to feel the shift.

That shift is the real beginning of being useful on this project.

Welcome to the unlearning.

It is hard work, but it is some of the most important work we will do together.

 @VoidStateKate @sierracatalina

High-power semiconductor, fiber, and related lasers for aerospace, defense, industrial, and microfabrication applications:

$LASR (nLIGHT) — Closest match: High-power semiconductor + fiber lasers for aerospace/defense and industrial microfabrication.
$IPGP (IPG Photonics) — Leading high-power fiber laser maker, strong in industrial + advanced applications.
$COHR (Coherent Corp.) — Broad laser portfolio including high-power fiber/diode systems for industrial, microfabrication, and defense.
$LITE (Lumentum) — High-power lasers and photonics components with industrial and high-performance applications.
$LASE (Laser Photonics) — Industrial laser systems, including cleaning and materials processing with defense overlap.
$MKSI (MKS Instruments) — Photonics and laser components/systems (broader exposure but relevant to high-power and precision applications).

text:
Tower, Marvell pass five million coherent photonic ICs

Tower Semiconductor (NASDAQ/TASE: TSEM) and Marvell Technology have shipped more than five million coherent photonic integrated circuits built on Tower's silicon photonics platform, the Israeli foundry said Thursday from Migdal Haemek. The chips are used in coherent optical modules for data center interconnects, where they support higher bandwidth and lower power per bit than legacy approaches. Tower manufactures the photonic ICs at its fabs and supplies them to Marvell, which integrates them into its coherent digital signal processor-based optical products. Neither company disclosed the value of the shipments or named end customers. (Tower Semiconductor)

Vik:
This announcement is only one thing: a flex to show who is building more SiPho chips right now. Googled "GF shipments SiPho" and came up with 3 substacks (one was mine). Flex is working.

"text": "Tower, Marvell pass five million coherent photonic ICs Tower Semiconductor (NASDAQ/TASE: TSEM) and Marvell Technology have shipped more than five million coherent photonic integrated circuits built on Tower's silicon photonics platform, the Israeli foundry said Thursday from Migdal Haemek. The chips are used in coherent optical modules for data center interconnects, where they support higher bandwidth and lower power per bit than legacy approaches. Tower manufactures the photonic ICs at its fabs and supplies them to Marvell, which integrates them into its coherent digital signal processor-based optical products. Neither company disclosed the value of the shipments or named end customers. (Tower Semiconductor) Vik: This announcement is only one thing: a flex to show who is building more SiPho chips right now. Googled "GF shipments SiPho" and came up with 3 substacks (one was mine). text:
7/
Net view:

Power and memory are still binding constraints today.

Interconnects and silicon photonics are the next layer the market is beginning to price in.

The companies best positioned sit at the intersection of InP lasers ( $LITE , $COHR ), advanced packaging ( $TSM ), and AI networking demand ( $NVDA , $MRVL , $AVGO ).

2026 is shaping up as the first year of meaningful commercial CPO ramps. The supply chain is tight, but the direction is clear: light is replacing copper for the highest-bandwidth links in AI infrastructure.

Not financial advice. This is for informational purposes only.

5/
$TSM COUPE is emerging as the central integration platform.
•  65nm silicon photonics base already in volume production
•  COUPE (Compact Universal Photonic Engine) uses advanced stacking to combine electrical and photonic dies
•  Targeted for meaningful CPO volume in 2026
•  $NVDA is already shipping early TSMC-backed CPO switches

As CPO moves from pluggables into co-packaged and deeper integration, TSMC’s packaging capacity becomes a key gating factor.

text:
3/
The silicon photonics supply chain (AI data center focus):

Upstream
•  SOI wafers (base substrate)
•  Indium Phosphide (InP) — the critical material for high-performance lasers. No scalable substitute exists today.

Lasers & Active Components
•  InP-based EMLs and continuous-wave lasers
•  Key names: $LITE (Lumentum) and $COHR (Coherent)

Design & Fabrication
•  Photonic Integrated Circuits (PICs)
•  $TSM (TSMC) COUPE platform is currently leading for AI-relevant integration

Advanced Packaging & CPO
•  This is becoming the new bottleneck layer
•  TSMC CoWoS + COUPE stacking for co-packaged optics

Downstream
•  High-speed transceivers (800G/1.6T+)
•  Optical switches and fabrics (NVIDIA, Broadcom, Marvell) $NVDA $MRVL $AVGO

text:
2/
Why this layer matters now:

As AI clusters grow to tens of thousands of GPUs, the amount of data that needs to move between chips explodes. When accelerators sit idle waiting for data, the economics break.

Silicon photonics moves data with light instead of electricity. It delivers higher bandwidth density, lower power per bit, and longer reach than copper.
The shift from traditional pluggable optics to CPO is accelerating in 2026.

text:
$CRDO vs $AVGO – Analysis Summary
Based on the referenced Zacks Article 

The article favors CRDO over AVGO at present due to stronger growth, earnings
estimate revisions, and AI networking exposure.

Bullish Factors for CRDO
• Fiscal 2026 revenue grew more than 200% year-over-year.
• Management expects strong growth into fiscal 2027.
• Strong demand for Active Electrical Cables (AECs) from hyperscalers and AI infrastructure providers.
• Dust Photonics acquisition expands optical connectivity capabilities.
• High gross margins and operating leverage support profitability.

Why AVGO Remains Attractive
• More diversified business model.
• Significant cash flow generation.
• Strong AI customer relationships.
• Generally viewed as the lower-risk alternative.

Comparison
Growth Rate: CRDO Advantage
AI Networking Exposure: CRDO Advantage
Stability & Diversification: AVGO Advantage
Cash Flow Strength: AVGO Advantage
Upside Potential: CRDO Advantage
Downside Protection: AVGO Advantage

Perspective
The article reinforces the thesis that CRDO is evolving into a major AI networking platform.

Continued success in AECs, optical connectivity, and integration of Dust Photonics could support
ongoing growth. For covered-call investors, the primary risk is that the stock continues to outperform and requires rolling calls higher to avoid assignment.

Strategy
Premium members only…(omitted any further strategy)

The key question is whether CRDO's earnings growth will justify substantially.

text:
If I had to build a watchlist for the next phase of the AI supercycle, this would be it:

🚀 AI Compute

$NVDA – The king of AI chips.
$AVGO – AI networking + custom silicon powerhouse.
$AMD – Growing AI competitor with massive upside.

💾 Memory

$MU – The biggest winner from the HBM memory boom.

💡 Photonics & Optical Networking (My Favorite Theme Right Now)

$LITE – Optical components for AI data centers.
$COHR – Silicon photonics and laser technology.
$ANET – AI networking infrastructure leader.
$MRVL – Custom AI chips and optical connectivity.
$AAOI – High-speed optical transceivers.
$FN – Manufactures optical networking equipment.
$CIEN – Next-generation optical networking.

☁️ AI Infrastructure

$CRWV – AI cloud infrastructure.
$NBIS – AI-native cloud platform.
$IREN – AI data centers and GPU infrastructure.

🤖 AI Platforms

$MSFT – Enterprise AI leader.
$GOOG – AI search and cloud giant.
$META – AI models, ads, and smart glasses.
$AMZN – AI cloud and automation.

⚛️ Quantum (Long-Term Moonshots)

$IONQ
$RGTI
$QBTS

🚀 Space & AI

$RKLB – Space infrastructure meets AI.

⭐ My highest-conviction names

$NVDA • $AVGO • $ANET • $MRVL • $LITE • $COHR • $MU • $CRWV • $IONQ • $RKLB

These are the companies I’d keep on a watchlist because they sit at the center of the next AI infrastructure buildout—from compute to memory, networking, photonics, cloud, and eventually quantum.

text:
One of the biggest investment opportunities of this decade is quietly taking shape, and most investors still haven’t noticed.

Photonics today feels a lot like memory did a year ago. That’s exactly why we’re still early.

If you’ve followed the AI supercycle since 2022, the pattern is becoming impossible to ignore.

🚀 Phase 1: Chips

When ChatGPT changed the world, AI demand exploded. NVIDIA’s Hopper H100 and Blackwell GPUs became the backbone of AI infrastructure, while AMD steadily gained ground with its own GPUs and custom AI chips.

💾 Phase 2: Memory

As AI models grew larger, High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) became the next bottleneck. Demand surged, supply tightened, and companies like Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung experienced powerful re-ratings as pricing power returned. Today, memory is in the middle of a prolonged supercycle, with capacity sold out years in advance.

💡 Phase 3: Photonics

Now the next bottleneck is emerging.

AI data centers are pushing against the limits of electricity, heat, bandwidth, and latency. Traditional electrical connections simply can’t keep up.

That’s where silicon photonics (SiPh) and co-packaged optics (CPO) come in.

Instead of moving data with electrical signals, they use light—making AI clusters faster, more efficient, and significantly more power-friendly.

The industry is only beginning this transition:

• 1.6T optical transceivers are entering production.
• Early CPO deployments have started.
• Large-scale adoption is expected in 2027–2028 and beyond.
• Goldman Sachs expects the market opportunity to grow roughly 10x by the end of the decade.

Looking at the AI supercycle through three stages:

📈 Chips → Mature Growth (Phase 3)
📈 Memory → Late Acceleration (Phase 2/3)
📈 Photonics → Early Acceleration (Phase 2) 🔥

Every AI wave creates a new bottleneck, and every bottleneck creates a new investment opportunity.

First it was compute.
Then it was memory.
Now it’s connectivity.

The world isn’t asking for less AI—it needs exponentially more compute, more bandwidth, and more efficiency.

That’s why photonics could become one of the defining investment themes of the second half of this decade.

At this point, calling this market “bullish” almost feels bearish.

text:
Our strategy must be smarter. Our true competitive advantage is our massive digital market with ~13 Billion Monthly UPI Transactions processing ~$240 Billion USD.
​This frictionless economy generates behavioral data of unprecedented quality. This data is our bargaining chip in the new negotiation technology landscape. Our goal is not a self-sufficient ecosystem, but an irreplaceable one in the global value chain.
Strategic Alliances are Key: We must stop relying on the west and build a warehouse of specialized non-US allies.
​🔗 Japan: Optical Computing & Advanced Photonics.
🔗 South Korea: High-Density Data Center Comms & Power Highways.
🔗 Taiwan: Advanced Chip Manufacturing.
🔗 Europe (e.g., Swiss/Netherlands): High-Precision Machines.

text:
I spent 2 hours of my Saturday reviewing hundreds of charts. These are the setups that stood out and what you should focus on this week.

Small caps are leading. Semiconductors are trying to reclaim leadership. Most of the Magnificent 7 still look tired.

$IWM closed at new highs.
$SMTC may be one of the cleanest setups on the board.
$SPCX enters its first full week as a public company.

Here’s the watchlist and recording:

$SPX: Strong bounce off the 50-day. Reclaimed 7335-7340 and closed above the prior Friday low. Above 7450 opens a move toward 7520. Below 7400 puts pressure back on the market.

$QQQ: Back near the highs. Above 724 keeps momentum intact and opens the door to a retest of all-time highs.

$IWM: One of the strongest charts right now. Closed at new highs around 293 and continues to lead.

$SMH: Semiconductors bounced hard and remain a key leadership group. Watching for continuation higher.

$BTC: Attempting to form a bottom, but still no clear setup. Needs more time.

$MSFT: Failed after the move toward the 200-day. No compelling setup here for now.

$META: Broke below 600 and continues to look vulnerable. Could see further downside if buyers don't step in soon.

$AMZN: Still holding above the 200-day. Watching 243 and 250 for signs of strength.

$AAPL: Clear bear flag developing. Relative weakness remains compared to the broader market.

$NVDA: Struggling below key moving averages. No clean setup at the moment.

$TSLA: Better than most mega caps. Closed near highs Friday. Watching how it behaves alongside $SPCX.

$GOOGL: Lower highs and below key moving averages. Needs a catalyst to regain momentum.

$SPCX: First full week as a public company. Watching the IPO range closely. Above the highs could trigger momentum. Below 150 would be a warning sign.

$ROKU: Huge move on strategic acquisition headlines. Above 150 keeps momentum alive.

$ARM: One of the stronger semiconductor charts. Holding 380 and breaking 385 could trigger another leg higher.

$AXTI: Photonics remains interesting. Watching 104 for continuation.

$INTC: One of the strongest semis on Friday. Above 128 could trigger a meaningful breakout.

$WDC: Filled the gap and recovered well. Watching for continuation above 572.

$SNDK: Excellent relative strength. Needs to reclaim and hold above 2000.

$MU: Lagging behind SNDK. Watching 1000 and 1015 closely.

$AMD: Strong bounce. Holding 500 and reclaiming 510-520 could open a move toward highs.

$NBIS: One of the more interesting setups. Above 240 could trigger fresh momentum.

$QCOM: Double-bottom style setup near range lows. Looking for rotation back toward highs.

CAVA: Quiet recovery underway. Looks capable of making a run toward 100.

$WOLF: Strong relative strength. Above 26.5 remains interesting.

$RL: New highs. Watching 400 hold and continuation above 405.

$AMAT: Clean continuation setup. Watching prior-day highs.

$GS: Improving again. Above 1080 opens the door to 1100.

$JPM: One of the stronger financial setups. Watching 322.

$SMTC: One of my favorite charts right now. Watching 170-173 for a breakout.

$BAC: Quietly approaching new highs.

$CAT: Rotation candidate if money moves away from tech. Watching 920-930.

$C: New highs and one of the strongest bank charts.

$HLT / $MAR: Hotels continue to show strength and sit near highs.

$LRCX: Strong trend remains intact. Watching above 370.

$HOOD: Strong recovery. Watching 95-96 for continuation.

$GE: Constructive hammer candle. Looks capable of making another run at highs.

$MRVL: One of the better semiconductor setups. Above 300 could accelerate quickly.

$UNH: Quiet strength. Watching continuation toward 440-445.

SNOW: Pulling back after a strong run. Needs time to reset.

$RKLB: Space trade cooled significantly after the $SPCX IPO. Needs stabilization.

$ASTS: Similar story. Heavy pullback after a huge run into the IPO.

Overall theme:

The market bounced, but confirmation is still needed.

IWM, semiconductors, memory names, and financials are showing the best relative strength.

Most of the Magnificent 7 continue to lag and remain below key moving averages.

SMTC, INTC, SNDK, NBIS, MRVL, SPCX, and JPM are some of my favorite charts going into next week.

If you like this, then like it ❤️

text:
Our strategy must be smarter. Our true competitive advantage is our massive digital market with ~13 Billion Monthly UPI Transactions processing ~$240 Billion USD.
​This frictionless economy generates behavioral data of unprecedented quality. This data is our bargaining chip in the new negotiation technology landscape. Our goal is not a self-sufficient ecosystem, but an irreplaceable one in the global value chain.
Strategic Alliances are Key: We must stop relying on the west and build a warehouse of specialized non-US allies.
​🔗 Japan: Optical Computing & Advanced Photonics.
🔗 South Korea: High-Density Data Center Comms & Power Highways.
🔗 Taiwan: Advanced Chip Manufacturing.
🔗 Europe (e.g., Swiss/Netherlands): High-Precision Machines.

text:
That is a very poor conclusion.
The central argument is that the US government’s strategy is fundamentally short-sighted. Ironically, American AI data centers would not function without European leadership in photonics. Anyone who has paid even modest attention to this sector should understand that by now.
But I don’t want to get drawn into the tired debate about who is dependent on whom.

As for whether a market of 449 million consumers matters, that is ultimately for business leaders to decide.

text:
Our strategy must be smarter. Our true competitive advantage is our massive digital market with ~13 Billion Monthly UPI Transactions processing ~$240 Billion USD.
​This frictionless economy generates behavioral data of unprecedented quality. This data is our bargaining chip in the new negotiation technology landscape. Our goal is not a self-sufficient ecosystem, but an irreplaceable one in the global value chain.
Strategic Alliances are Key: We must stop relying on the west and build a warehouse of specialized non-US allies.
​🔗 Japan: Optical Computing & Advanced Photonics.
🔗 South Korea: High-Density Data Center Comms & Power Highways.
🔗 Taiwan: Advanced Chip Manufacturing.
🔗 Europe (e.g., Swiss/Netherlands): High-Precision Machines.

text:
India’s AI Moat is NOT Sovereign Models. It’s Sovereign Data.

​As India rapidly digitizes, sophisticated AI-driven cyber attacks pose a threat to our critical infrastructure far beyond WhatsApp vulnerabilities. The era of trusting external tech partners is over.
​The prevailing wisdom is to build internal state capacity: sovereign models, native research. But the math doesn’t work. We lack the $300M+ GPU clusters and hyperscale data centers required for foundation LLM training.
​Our strategy must be smarter. Our true competitive advantage is our massive digital market with ~13 Billion Monthly UPI Transactions processing ~$240 Billion USD.
​This frictionless economy generates behavioral data of unprecedented quality. This data is our bargaining chip in the new negotiation technology landscape. Our goal is not a self-sufficient ecosystem, but an irreplaceable one in the global value chain.
​How we win: We must build a trust layer to monetize this data securely. At Equidei, our Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZK) based ML pipeline is designed for exactly this moment. By placing public hashes on a Distributed Ledger (DLT) with Smart Contracts, we enable trustless data sharing. Users can share insights derived from data, but no raw data access is granted, and no copies are made. This creates a secure "Federated Data" model.
​Strategic Alliances are Key: We must stop relying on the west and build a warehouse of specialized non-US allies.
​🔗 Japan: Optical Computing & Advanced Photonics.
🔗 South Korea: High-Density Data Center Comms & Power Highways.
🔗 Taiwan: Advanced Chip Manufacturing.
🔗 Europe (e.g., Swiss/Netherlands): High-Precision Machines.  
​India secures this Asia AI Warehouse by providing the one thing nobody else has: the massive behavioral data repository, verified and secured by a ZK-trust layer. This is how we build irreplacable capacity, secure our digital economy, and maintain true sovereignty.

text:
I think one of the biggest underappreciated stories from the new EU Chips Act 2.0 isn't another mega fab. It's photonics. For years it felt like a niche research field. Now Europe is effectively treating it as strategic infrastructure. Why?

Because AI is running into a data movement problem. Copper is becoming the bottleneck, so the next wave is Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) and silicon photonics. That changes the investment story.

1) Soitec $SOI : The EU’s official impact analysis explicitly named Soitec, noting that "The EU has a relatively strong global position in SOI wafers, with Soitec... being notable players." Because optical transceivers and high-bandwidth optical interconnects are physically manufactured on Silicon Photonics-SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator) substrates, Soitec sits at the absolute genesis of the raw material supply chain.
In addition, Soitec is already deeply integrated into European microelectronics funding tracks (like the IPCEI ME/CT project), leading key workstreams in communications infrastructure. The Chips Act 2.0 ensures that Soitec's next-generation wafer expansion (like SmartSiC and specialized optical/RF substrates) will receive heavily subsidized structural runway.

2) X-FAB $XFAB is the pure-play specialty foundry leading the European Silicon Photonics and heterogeneous integration manufacturing value chain.
XFAB was also named in the impact analysis and qualifies for the prized FOAK (First-of-a-Kind) category. This means the company is positioned to receive streamlined state-aid approvals and direct EU grants to expand its European factories for next-generation silicon photonics modules.
$XFAB is the direct winner of the physical infrastructure side of Chips Act 2.0. The act’s specific focus on strengthening "production technologies including co-packaging and heterogeneous integration with electronic chips" is effectively a targeted mandate for what X-FAB does.

3) Sivers ($SIVE) is the higher-beta bet. 
The proposal explicitly highlights funding for "design libraries and design automation tools for photonic integrated circuits" and "co-packaged optics for AI data centres." Because Sivers operates a "fab-lite" model, the policy helps subsidize the complex R&D, design, and prototyping phases of their customer pipelines (e.g., Ayar Labs, Jabil, and Marvell). Furthermore, Sivers was recently integrated directly into GlobalFoundries’ ($GFS) SCALE™ CPO platform blueprints as a foundry reference design. Subsidizing European pilot lines for CPO means European systems builders will face lower friction adopting Sivers-backed laser architectures.
While the technology is deeply validated, $SIVE is in the middle of a brutal tug-of-war. Short-selling reports (e.g., Ningi Research), class-action shareholder lawsuits regarding listing delays, and heavy corporate insider selling have crushed governance credibility. 

So, the EU policy guarantees a robust, long-term industry tailwind for Sivers' technology, but near-term execution and corporate governance remain highly volatile.

As a forward-looking investor, I expect the market to price in these macro tailwinds well before physical cash flows reflect them. If Europe is serious about AI sovereignty, photonics is no longer optional. The market may not wait for revenues to show up before pricing that in.

We should expect *3 to 6 Months for  Legislative Passage & State-Aid Framework to finalized.
*Then 6 to 12 Months: Consortiums Formed and Direct EU Grants allocated
*Finally, 12 to 15+ Months: Revenue & Capital Expenditure expansion manifests.

Personally, I see:
• Lower-risk infrastructure: $SOI, $XFAB
• Higher-risk, higher-upside: $SIVE
The bottleneck is shifting from compute to connectivity. That's where I'm spending my research time.

text:
One of the BEST investment opportunities of the decade is unfolding right now. Most people still don’t see it.

Photonics is where Memory was a year ago. That’s why we’re still VERY early.

The pattern is clear if you’ve been watching this AI super cycle unfold since 2022.

> Chips kicked everything off after ChatGPT dropped in late 2022/early 2023. $NVDA went nuclear with Hopper H100s, and then Blackwell came. $AMD started carving out real share in GPUs and custom ASICs.

> Memory followed with a lag. Mid-2024 onward, HBM went into massive shortage as AI training and inference sucked up capacity. That pulled DRAM and NAND higher too. Micron $MU, SK Hynix, Samsung and other memory players delivered strong re-ratings as pricing power returned.

> Now in mid-2026, memory is deep in its prolonged supercycle - sold out for years ahead (confirmed by Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra). Chips are in a more mature phase, still growing strong with 1:1 CPU to GPU ratios as AI agents scale, and will sustain into the second half of this decade as Lisa Su says compute capacity will 100x by 2030.

But what’s next? 

Photonics, folks! 

- AI clusters are suffering from constraints related to power, heat, latency and bandwidth. Optics solves this. That is why the next MAJOR leg is clearly SiPh and CPO.

- 1.6T pluggables are ramping, early CPO deployments are starting but nowhere near to the scale they will reach because real mass adoption and deep in-package integration will come in 2027-2028+.

- Goldman Sach predicts TAM will explode 10x by the end of this decade. 

To better understand this, if one divides it into 3 common phases across the supercycles (Early, Acceleration, Maturity), we see that:

- Chips are clearly in Phase 3
- Memory is in Phase 2/3
- Photonics is very early in Phase 2 🔥

The AI buildout keeps shifting to the next bottleneck because the world needs more compute than ever before. This isn’t slowing down anytime soon!

BULLISH seems like a bearish word now.

Most people still don’t see it. Photonics is where Memory was a year ago. That’s why we’re still VERY early. The pattern is clear if you’ve been watching this AI super cycle unfold since 2022. > Chips kicked everything off after ChatGPT dropped in late 2022/early 2023. $NVDA went nuclear with Hopper H100s, and then Blackwell came. $AMD started carving out real share in GPUs and custom ASICs. > Memory followed with a lag. Mid-2024 onward, HBM went into massive shortage as AI training and inference sucked up capacity. That pulled DRAM and NAND higher too. Micron $MU, SK Hynix, Samsung and other memory players delivered strong re-ratings as pricing power returned. > Now in mid-2026, memory is deep in its prolonged supercycle - sold out for years ahead (confirmed by Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra). Chips are in a more mature phase, still growing strong with 1:1 CPU to GPU ratios as AI agents scale, and will sustain into the second half of this decade as Lisa Su says compute capacity will 100x by 2030. But what’s next? Photonics, folks! - AI clusters are suffering from constraints related to power, heat, latency and bandwidth. Optics solves this. That is why the next MAJOR leg is clearly SiPh and CPO. - 1.6T pluggables are ramping, early CPO deployments are starting but nowhere near to the scale they will reach because real mass adoption and deep in-package integration will come in 2027-2028+. - Goldman Sach predicts TAM will explode 10x by the end of this decade. To better understand this, if one divides it into 3 common phases across the supercycles (Early, Acceleration, Maturity), we see that: - Chips are clearly in Phase 3 - Memory is in Phase 2/3 - Photonics is very early in Phase 2 🔥 The AI buildout keeps shifting to the next bottleneck because the world needs more compute than ever before. This isn’t slowing down anytime soon!
